name: crocus sativus
mechanism: Hypothesized mechanisms include serotonergic modulation and antioxidant/anti-inflammatory signaling; mechanistic certainty remains incomplete.
effects: Meta-analysis of randomized trials reports symptom improvements versus controls in depression/anxiety contexts.
Magnitude and certainty vary by extract standardization, comparator, and trial quality.
contraindications: Use caution in pregnancy and in bipolar-spectrum conditions unless supervised by qualified clinicians.
safetyNotes: Short-term controlled use is often tolerated, but concentrated extracts can differ from culinary exposure.
description: Crocus sativus (saffron) is a culinary and medicinal spice with modern randomized-trial syntheses for mood-related outcomes.
dosage: Dose-response depends on standardized extract and indication; no single cross-product dose is appropriate.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. interactions: Potential interactions with serotonergic or CNS-active medicines require medication-level review.
preparation: Used as culinary stigma material and as standardized oral extracts in clinical research.
region: Cultivated historically in Iran and the Mediterranean, now distributed globally.
traditionalUse: Traditional Persian and Mediterranean medical systems include saffron for mood and general vitality support.
summary: Recent meta-analysis evidence suggests saffron preparations may improve depression and anxiety outcomes in some settings, though formulation and trial heterogeneity remain important limitations.

researchEnrichment.evidenceSummary: Indexability remediation update for crocus-sativus using curated registry sources and claim-level provenance.

supportedUses.claim: Recent meta-analysis evidence suggests saffron preparations may improve depression and anxiety outcomes in some settings, though formulation and trial heterogeneity remain important limitations.

unsupportedOrUnclearUses.claim: Evidence remains limited by study design heterogeneity and/or sparse direct human trials for this exact herb preparation.

mechanisms.claim: Hypothesized mechanisms include serotonergic modulation and antioxidant/anti-inflammatory signaling; mechanistic certainty remains incomplete.
mechanisms.uncertaintyNote: Mechanistic findings should not be interpreted as proven clinical efficacy.

interactions.claim: Potential interactions with serotonergic or CNS-active medicines require medication-level review.

contraindications.claim: Use caution in pregnancy and in bipolar-spectrum conditions unless supervised by qualified clinicians.

adverseEffects.claim: Short-term controlled use is often tolerated, but concentrated extracts can differ from culinary exposure.

dosageContextNotes.claim: Dose-response depends on standardized extract and indication; no single cross-product dose is appropriate.

researchGaps.claim: High-quality, formulation-specific human trials and longer-term safety follow-up are still needed.
